images.caption: Figure 1:Per-horizon optimal context-horizon relationships for four time series. The context lengths were obtained via hyperparameter search and are shown as color-matched bars.
Figure 2:Left: Median optimal lookback per (series, horizon) varies.Right: Adapting context per horizon yields up to +16% MSE improvement over a global baseline across these four datasets.

Figure 3:Optimal lookbackL∗L^{*}vs. forecast horizonHHacross 8 datasets. (a) Median lookback (log-log) with IQR bands and power-law fitsL∗∝HbL^{*}\propto H^{b}. (b) Fitted exponentbbper dataset. (c) Per-series exponent distribution within each dataset.

Figure 4:Effect of series (Top) and horizon (Bottom) grouping on forecasting accuracy across 4 datasets. Each panel shows MSE degradation (%) relative to the best group size (marked with⋆\star) as a function of group size, from per-series/horizon to fully shared.

Figure 5:Forecast comparison. Our method (blue) closely tracks the ground truth (black), while the global baseline (red) reverts to the mean, particularly on non-stationary series (Weather, Exchange). The choppiness in Weather and especially Exchange is an artifact of switching hyperparameters across forecast settings, which can create discontinuities between segments.

Figure 6:Weight magnitude|w||w|over lag and forecast horizon. Lighter shades indicate larger magnitudes. Lag is measured from the most recent input (bottom row); white regions lie beyond each model’s chosen lookback.
Figure 7:Per-series hyperparameter (local ratiorrand regularizationα\alpha) heatmaps, withgh=48g_{h}=48.

Figure 8:Augmentation analysis.Left: Proportion of trials selecting frequency-domain noise, time-domain noise, or no augmentation.Right: Distribution of optimalσ\sigmaconditional on augmentation being selected, broken down by domain.

header: Appendix ALong-range linear autocorrelation in the benchmark series

images.caption: Figure 9:Channel-averaged Pearson autocorrelationρ^​(yt,yt−k)\hat{\rho}(y_{t},y_{t-k})as a function of lagkk, for the six standard benchmarks under four deseasonalization schemes. Dotted vertical line marksk=720k=720. The confidence intervals are narrow (typical95%95\%bootstrap half-width below0.010.01, given in Table2fork=720k=720). The four schemes agree at long lags, with all six datasets retainingρ^≥0.39\hat{\rho}\geq 0.39atk=720k=720after harmonic deseasonalization.

header: Appendix BHow much accuracy comes from context length alone

images.caption: Figure 10:Median per-channel test MSE on Ridge with universal-default preprocessing atH=720H=720as a function of the lookbackLL. Lines are color-coded by regime. Strong (blue) continues to improve up toL=2000L=2000, plateau (orange) saturates aroundL=192L=192–336336, and flat (gray) is essentially insensitive toLL.
